title: Incursa.Quic Public API Surface Verification
verification_method: Execution, inspection, and API-diff evidence from the public API analyzer, plus targeted surface tests derived from the Microsoft public-surface behavior baseline and the existing connection/runtime seams.
preconditions: The approved SPEC-QUIC-API and ARC-QUIC-API-0001 artifacts exist.
The Incursa.Quic project builds with public API analyzer support enabled.
The consumer facade types are available for inspection or promotion.
The Microsoft reference tests used as behavioral evidence have been reviewed and mapped to the refined surface expectations.
scope: This verification slice covers the connection-, listener-, first client-entry, narrow stream-entry, supported write/completion plus narrow reset/stop-sending abort pair, and narrow initial stream-capacity consumer public API surface, the close/dispose/error projection visible through that surface, the server-side listener validation and accept semantics, the supported positive client/server loopback establishment boundary, the supported positive stream open/accept boundary on that same active loopback path, the supported write/completion plus reset/stop-sending abort boundary on that same active loopback path, the supported initial outbound stream-capacity callback boundary on that same path, the pending client-connect validation/cancellation/terminal semantics, and the internal-only boundary. It proves that only the approved facade types are public, that `QuicStreamType` has the direction-only shape, that `QuicListener` and `QuicListenerOptions` honor validation, cancellation, and disposal, that `QuicClientConnectionOptions` and `QuicConnection.ConnectAsync(...)` expose only the approved shape, that `ConnectAsync(...)`, `AcceptInboundStreamAsync(...)`, `OpenOutboundStreamAsync(...)`, and the supported `QuicStream` write/completion plus narrow abort pair return real `QuicConnection` and `QuicStream` facades only after the supported boundaries are reached, that combined `Abort(Both, ...)` is rejected explicitly, that the supported stream-capacity callback subset reports only non-zero initial peer-advertised outbound stream-open increments, and that the listed helper/runtime seam types remain internal. It does not attempt to prove full TLS-auth parity, later `MAX_STREAMS`-driven capacity updates, broad stream-management completeness, wire behavior beyond the active loopback path, or performance.
expected_result: The public API matches the approved connection-, listener-, client-entry, narrow stream-entry, supported write/completion plus narrow reset/stop-sending abort pair, and narrow initial stream-capacity consumer surface, the close/dispose/error projection is visible, `QuicStreamType` has the approved direction-only shape, the listener validation and accept semantics are honest, the supported positive loopback establishment boundary is real, the supported positive stream open/accept boundary is real, the supported write/completion plus reset/stop-sending abort boundary is real, combined `Abort(Both, ...)` is rejected explicitly, the supported initial stream-capacity callback boundary is real, pending client connect is honest about cancellation and unsupported TLS settings, and the helper/runtime seam types remain internal.
procedure: Compare src/Incursa.Quic/PublicAPI.Unshipped.txt and any future API approval diffs against the approved type list.
Run compile-time or reflection checks that the approved public types and members are present.
Run negative access checks that the helper and runtime seam types cannot be referenced from outside the assembly.

Add or review focused consumer-behavior tests for `QuicStreamType` shape, close/dispose/error mapping, read-side and write-side terminal behavior, listener validation, accept cancellation/disposal, positive client/server loopback establishment, supported stream open/accept on the active loopback path, supported write/completion plus `Abort(Read, ...)` / `Abort(Write, ...)` on that path, combined `Abort(Both, ...)` rejection, supported initial stream-capacity callback invocation, client option/connect validation, pending-connect cancellation/disposal, unsupported TLS rejection, and facade-to-runtime/host mapping.
Review the design note, spec, and the reference Microsoft test evidence together to confirm no extra public abstractions were introduced.
status_summary: Execution evidence now covers the supported positive client/server loopback boundary, the listener/server slice, the connected-session facade, the supported positive stream open/accept boundary on the active loopback path, the supported write/completion plus narrow reset/stop-sending abort pair on that same path, and the supported initial stream-capacity callback subset on that same path. The proof target includes client option/connect validation, unsupported TLS rejection, combined `Abort(Both, ...)` rejection, terminal cancellation/disposal behavior, and the narrow stream-entry, write/completion, abort-pair, plus initial stream-capacity boundaries; full TLS-auth parity and later capacity evolution remain out of scope.

supplemental_sections.heading: Evidence Notes
supplemental_sections.content: The current proof includes build output and focused requirement-home tests for `REQ-QUIC-API-0001`, `REQ-QUIC-API-0002`, `REQ-QUIC-API-0003`, `REQ-QUIC-API-0004`, `REQ-QUIC-API-0005`, `REQ-QUIC-API-0006`, `REQ-QUIC-API-0008`, `REQ-QUIC-API-0009`, and `REQ-QUIC-API-0010`, including the supported positive loopback establishment, stream-entry, supported write/completion plus reset/stop-sending abort pair, combined `Abort(Both, ...)` rejection, and initial stream-capacity callback tests. The remaining evidence addendum work is now focused on broader TLS/auth parity, combined abort parity, and later stream-capacity evolution.

supplemental_sections.heading: Reference Behavioral Evidence
supplemental_sections.content:
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\QuicListenerTests.cs`
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\QuicConnectionTests.cs`
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\QuicStreamTests.cs`
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\QuicStreamConnectedStreamConformanceTests.cs`
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\MsQuicCipherSuitesPolicyTests.cs`
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\MsQuicTests.cs`

These tests are not target-repo proof, but they define the baseline public-behavior expectations that the target verification plan should mirror.
